column1:
The preferred imaging plane to measure the AP dimension of the aorta is:

A: coronal
B: transverse
C: sagittal
D: radial
column2:
Answer: C. sagittal

Feedback:
The sagittal plane is the preferred imaging plane to measure the AP dimension of the aorta.

column1:
What cardiac complications are associated with the findings on the image?

A: cardiac tamponade or constrictive pericarditis
B: coronary artery disease or aortic stenosis
C: mitral stenosis or pulmonary stenosis
D: aortic regurgitation or pulmonary regurgitation
column2:
Answer: A. cardiac tamponade or constrictive pericarditis

Feedback:
Pulsus Alternans - the peak systolic velocity alternates between two levels with each heartbeat. Pulsus Alternans is associated with cardiac tamponade, dilated cardiomyopathy and constrictive pericarditis.

column1:
Which of the following is not a characteristic of a high-grade stenosis?

A: Loss of pulsatility of waveform distally
B: Laminar flow just distal to the site of stenosis
C: Spectral broadening just distal to the site of stenosis
D: Extremely high velocity signals at the site of stenosis
column2:
Answer: B. Laminar flow just distal to the site of stenosis

Feedback:
Turbulent flow is demonstrated distal to a stenosis of the vessel. Spectral broadening occurs due to the variety of blood cell velocities present and this results in non-laminar flow patterns.

column1:
Which of the following statements correctly describes the image of the aorta?

A: The total AP dimension of the AAA size is the distance between the actual vessel walls.
B: Embolism formation has occurred in the area of dilatation due to swirling and stasis of blood.
C: The AAA shown requires immediate treatment due to its location proximal to the renal arteries.
D: The true lumen is the distance between the actual vessel walls.
column2:
Answer: A. The total AP dimension of the AAA size is the distance between the actual vessel walls.

Feedback:
It is important to measure the AAA size and the actual lumen size, if thrombus accumulation is present within the AAA. The true lumen is the anechoic space in the middle of the vessel. The total AP dimension of the AAA size is the distance between the actual vessel walls. The AAA demonstrates thrombus accumulation along the walls. An embolism is a moving thrombus that gets trapped in a distal smaller vessel. Embolisms are very rarely seen travelling in a vessel.

column1:
A patient presents with diminished pulses in the right radial and ulnar arteries. Which of the following could cause this symptom?

A: right brachial artery stenosis
B: right superficial palmar arch stenosis
C: right deep palmar arch stenosis
D: right lateral palmar arch stenosis
column2:
Answer: A. right brachial artery stenosis

Feedback:
Reduced flow in the forearm arteries indicates a proximal stenosis. The palmar arch is in the hand, distal to the radial and ulnar arteries. Any artery proximal to the arteries with monophasic flow could be obstructed and cause the abnormal flow in the forearm arteries. This includes the brachial, axillary and subclavian arteries.

column1:
What is caput medusae?

A: lower extremity varicosities on the anterior knee
B: tortuous vessels around the umbilicus caused by portal HTN
C: lower extremity varicosities on the anterior thigh
D: formation of gastroesophageal varices caused by portal HTN
column2:
Answer: B. tortuous vessels around the umbilicus caused by portal HTN

Feedback:
Caput medusae refers to dilated, tortuous vessels around the umbilicus caused by portal HTN. The vessels are visible just beneath the subcutaneous tissue, similar to varicosities in the legs

column1:
A patient presents with intermittent left lower leg swelling for several months. The popliteal vein demonstrates a collapsed appearance with echogenic, thickened walls. These findings are most consistent with:

A: acute femoral DVT
B: proximal venous stenosis
C: vasculitis of the popliteal vein
D: chronic popliteal DVT
column2:
Answer: D. chronic popliteal DVT

Feedback:
Chronic DVT will become echogenic as it becomes more fibrous while attached to the vein wall. The fibrous material limits the pliability of the walls leading to a semi-collapsed appearance. Acute femoral DVT would cause a dilated popliteal vein.

column1:
A patient in the ER has just been diagnosed with acute DVT of the popliteal vein. What would be the physician's preferred treatment plan?

A: Insert a valvulotome into the popliteal vein and recanalize the lumen.
B: Begin the use of compression stockings and perform a follow up exam in 30 days
C: Immediate IV treatment with Heparin with 90 days of Warfarin medication once released from the hospital.
D: Perform sclerotherapy to resolve the clot.
column2:
Answer: C. Immediate IV treatment with Heparin with 90 days of Warfarin medication once released from the hospital.

Feedback:
Acute DVT is usually initially treated with IV Heparin followed by at least 90 days of Warfarin/Coumadin therapy once the patient is out of the hospital.

column1:
Which of the following are collagen disorders that have increased risk for arterial dissection?

A: Marfan syndrome and Polycythemia vera
B: Paget Schroetter syndrome and Raynaud syndrome
C: Shone complex and Blue Toe syndrome
D: Marfan syndrome and Ehler-Danlos syndrome
column2:
Answer: D. Marfan syndrome and Ehler-Danlos syndrome

Feedback:
Marfan syndrome and Ehler-Danlos syndrome are collagen disorders that can lead to the formation of arterial aneurysm or dissection. Shone complex is a group of cardiac defects and defects of the aortic arch and valve. Blue Toe syndrome is caused by emboli from an AAA with mural thrombus, Paget Schroetter syndrome affects the upper extremity veins. Raynaud syndrome is a collagen disorder that reduces flow to the hands/feet. Polycythemia vera is a rare cancer that causes blood thickening,

column1:
By dividing the pulsatility index proximal to a stenosis by the pulsatility index from distal to a stenosis, you can evaluate the:

A: intraluminal diameter
B: damping factor
C: intraluminal area
D: Reynolds number
column2:
Answer: B. damping factor

Feedback:
The damping factor describes the degree of attenuation of the Doppler signal caused by the stenosis. The PI will decrease distal to the stenosis. The greater the stenosis, the lower the distal PI and greater the damping factor.

column1:
D-dimer levels are most accurate for predicting:

A: the risk of pulmonary embolism
B: the presence of DVT
C: the risk of clot formation
D: the absence of DVT
column2:
Answer: D. the absence of DVT

Feedback:
D-dimer levels are most accurate for predicting the absence of DVT. D-dimer levels can be elevated for many reasons including DVT, cancer and other diseases. If DVT is suspected but the D-dimer levels are low, DVT is most likely not present.

column1:
A patient presents for a venous insufficiency exam but is unable to stand for the exam. How will this affect your exam?

A: Venous reflux may not be demonstrated in veins where it is actually present.
B: No change as long as they can move their feet to perform the heel raises.
C: The PPG tracings will be increased in amplitude.
D: The segmental pressures will be falsely elevated.
column2:
Answer: A. Venous reflux may not be demonstrated in veins where it is actually present.

Feedback:
Standing increases hydrostatic pressure to better demonstrate the reflux. If the patient is supine, a false negative exam could be obtained because the augmentation response will be diminished without hydrostatic pressure. A seated patient will have bent knees and hip joints which can inhibit/reduce the flow response to augmentation.

column1:
Which gonadal vein empties directly into the IVC?

A: Right gonadal vein
B: Left gonadal vein
C: Right and left gonadal veins
D: Neither gonadal vein empties directly into the IVC
column2:
Answer: A. Right gonadal vein

Feedback:
The right gonadal vein empties into the IVC, while the left gonadal vein empties into the left renal vein.

column1:
When performing a PVR exam of the lower extremity arteries, how are the waveforms obtained?

A: Starting at the ankle on the right leg, proceed up through the right thigh before repeating the process with the left leg
B: Starting at both thighs and moving down through the cuffs on both legs simultaneously
C: Starting at both ankles and moving up through the cuffs on both legs simultaneously
D: Starting at the right thigh cuff and moving toward the right ankle cuff, then repeating the process with the left leg
column2:
Answer: B. Starting at both thighs and moving down through the cuffs on both legs simultaneously

Feedback:
A segmental pressure exam is performed on one leg at a time, while a PVR exam is performed on both legs simultaneously. Segmental pressures are taken at the ankle first and then move up the leg. PVR exams are performed from the thighs down to the ankle.

column1:
You are performing a carotid duplex on a patient that had a carotid endarterectomy 2 weeks ago. There is homogeneous material lining the ICA and bulb and Doppler demonstrates turbulence and elevated velocities. Which of the following is the most likely cause for these findings?

A: progression of atherosclerotic disease
B: residual blockage from suboptimal surgical techniques
C: neointimal hyperplasia
D: intimal flap and dissection
column2:
Answer: B. residual blockage from suboptimal surgical techniques

Feedback:
Within the first month post-op, restenosis is attributed to suboptimal surgical techniques. Narrowing over the first 24 months after CEA is attributed to neointimal hyperplasia. After 2 years, stenosis occurs due to progression of atherosclerotic disease.

column1:
The most accurate Doppler predictor of ICA stenosis is:

A: the peak systolic velocity
B: the systolic ratio (ICAs/CCAs)
C: the end diastolic velocity
D: the diastolic ratio (ICAd/CCAd)
column2:
Answer: A. the peak systolic velocity

Feedback:
Recent studies have shown that the most accurate Doppler predictor of ICA stenosis is the peak systolic velocity.

column1:
Which of the following terms would be used to describe flow within an abdominal aortic aneurysm?

A: high resistance flow
B: non-laminar flow
C: plug flow
D: triphasic flow
column2:
Answer: B. non-laminar flow

Feedback:
Flow within the AAA will be decreased in resistance and turbulent due to the swirling effect in the enlarged segment. Normal aortic flow is called plug flow because the majority of the blood moves at the same velocity. Flow in a AAA would be of many different velocities and is considered non-laminar.

column1:
The images below are from a lower extremity arterial duplex examination. Review the information on the images and choose the waveform below that is most consistent with the expected waveform for the left popliteal artery. Image A Image B Image C Image D

A: Image C
B: Image D
C: Image B
D: Image A
column2:
Answer: D. Image A

Feedback:
The PTA waveform demonstrates monophasic, low resistance flow, but the ATA flow appears normal. This is most consistent with stenosis in the tibioperoneal trunk or an isolated stenosis in the proximal PTA. If the peroneal artery flow is normal = PTA stenosis. If the peroneal artery flow is abnormal = Tibioperoneal trunk stenosis. The popliteal artery should demonstrate a normal, high resistance flow pattern because it is proximal to the area of stenosis.

column1:
Which of the following describe a transcutaneous oximetry exam of the lower extremity?

A: The electrodes cool the tissues to 25 degrees Celsius to release oxygen.
B: Readings are taken in the toes, lower leg and thigh
C: A baseline reading is obtained first on the toes
D: It takes approximately 3 minutes to obtain a reading
column2:
Answer: B. Readings are taken in the toes, lower leg and thigh

Feedback:
Transcutaneous Oximetry determines capability of healing of a wound or specific site for amputation. The sensor evaluates oxygen supply and consumption in tissues surrounding the wound. Electrodes heat the skin to 45 degrees Celsius and oxygen escapes from the tissue to be measured by the sensor. It takes approximately 15-20 minutes to obtain a reading. System calibration is REQUIRED!!! First, obtain a reading on the chest as a baseline, then obtain readings of the toes, lower leg and thigh. Normal TcPO2 levels 70-80mmHg; <40mmHg indicates tissue hypoxia

column1:
Which intracranial artery courses within the sylvian fissure?

A: posterior cerebral artery
B: anterior cerebral artery
C: basilar artery
D: middle cerebral artery
column2:
Answer: D. middle cerebral artery

Feedback:
The M2 segment courses within the sylvian fissure toward the lateral aspect of the cranium.

column1:
Which of the following statements about the IVC is correct?

A: The IVC should normally maintain a relatively constant diameter with respiration.
B: The IVC is the most posterior vessel in the abdomen.
C: Congestive heart failure and pulmonary HTN can cause IVC dilatation.
D: The IVC empties blood into the left atrium.
column2:
Answer: C. Congestive heart failure and pulmonary HTN can cause IVC dilatation.

Feedback:
The IVC courses anterior to the level of the aorta and empties blood into the right atrium. The IVC should normally change diameter with respiration. The aorta maintains a constant diameter with respiration. Congestive heart failure , significant tricuspid regurgitation and pulmonary HTN can cause IVC dilatation.

column1:
The image demonstrates a complication that is most common with what type of carcinoma?

A: Hepatic
B: Renal
C: Splenic
D: Pancreatic
column2:
Answer: B. Renal

Feedback:
Renal carcinoma has been shown to infiltrate the renal vein and IVC causing mass and thrombus formation within. It is the most common type of tumor with IVC/renal vein invasion.

column1:
Which of the following is a limitation of the 2D evaluation of the extremity veins?

A: patient cannot lay flat for exam
B: location of the brachial vein
C: increased amount of fibrous tissues and ligaments in the adductor canal
D: inability to visualize the vessels immediately posterior to the inguinal ligament
column2:
Answer: C. increased amount of fibrous tissues and ligaments in the adductor canal

Feedback:
Limitations of venous duplex evaluations: Non-visualization of veins due to excessive transducer pressure Location of the subclavian vein beneath the clavicle increased amount of fibrous tissues and ligaments in the adductor canal Patient that cannot be semi-erect or standing for exam The inguinal ligament is not normally visible on ultrasound evaluation, and it does not cause non-visualization of adjacent anatomy. The adductor canal contains several ligaments and connective tissues that limit compression of the distal femoral vein. Increased transducer pressure can cause non-visualization of normal veins. The subclavian vein courses posterior to the clavicle, which limits manual compression. The superficial venous systems of the upper and lower extremities can be easily compressed causing non-visualization Patients for DVT exams should be scanned in the semierect position to dilate the veins for easier visualization. A supine patient can cause the veins to be difficult to identify. Insufficiency exams should be performed with the patient standing, to engorge the veins with blood and encourage the display of reflux. A supine patient could cause false negative results for reflux.

column1:
If the left proximal CCA demonstrates a PSV of 35cm/s and the right proximal CCA demonstrates a PSV of 115cm/s, which of the following could explain the findings?

A: decreased hematocrit levels
B: left ICA occlusion
C: aortic valve stenosis
D: congestive heart failure
column2:
Answer: B. left ICA occlusion

Feedback:
If a single CCA demonstrates elevated velocity = tortuosity on that side or compensatory flow due to obstruction in the other carotid system. If the left ICA is occluded, flow in the CCA will be low velocity with a quick upstroke and little to no diastolic flow. The right CCA velocity can increase as it tries to provide the additional cerebrovascular flow needed. The left vertebral artery may also demonstrate elevated velocity as it tries to compensate for the ipsilateral ICA occlusion. The other choices would cause bilateral hemodynamic changes.

column1:
Which vein courses from the lateral aspect of the wrist extending up the lateral arm to the shoulder to join the axillary vein?

A: cephalic
B: basilic
C: axillary
D: brachial
column2:
Answer: A. cephalic

Feedback:
The cephalic vein courses from the lateral aspect of the wrist extending up the lateral arm to the shoulder to join the axillary vein to form the subclavian vein.

column1:
A patient presents for evaluation of their hemodialysis graft. The proximal anastomosis demonstrates a velocity of 130cm/s. The mid graft velocity is 275cm/s and the distal anastomosis velocity is 50cm/s. What is your conclusion about the findings on the exam?

A: There is stenosis of the mid graft segment.
B: There is stenosis of the proximal anastomosis and the mid graft segment.
C: The Doppler evaluation is normal.
D: The venous outflow is most likely thrombosed.
column2:
Answer: A. There is stenosis of the mid graft segment.

Feedback:
Flow velocities that double between segments indicate graft stenosis. The velocity increases by more than 2X at the mid segment. The velocity at the proximal anastomosis is normal as flow within the grafts is usually high velocity. The distal graft velocity drops significantly distal to the stenosis. This also indicates a significant stenosis is present in the graft.

column1:
Which of the following indicates a significant SMA stenosis?

A: mesenteric - aortic ratio > 2.0
B: pain in the abdomen when hungry that is relieved by eating/digestion
C: PSV >200cm/s
D: dilated inferior mesenteric artery that is easily visualized sonographically
column2:
Answer: D. dilated inferior mesenteric artery that is easily visualized sonographically

Feedback:
The IMA is usually very tough to find in most patients with ultrasound. If the artery is visible and easily evaluated, it usually means it is dilated due to compensatory flow related to an SMA stenosis. Increased flow is being passed to the IMA due to the obstruction in the SMA. A PSV greater than 2.75cm/s indicates significant stenosis. If the mesenteric - aortic ratio exceeds 3.0, a significant stenosis is present. SMA disease causes pain after eating due to inadequate blood supply caused by the stenosis.

column1:
Which of the following is an advantage of intraoperative vascular ultrasound compared to intraoperative angiography?

A: Technique does not require vessel puncture to obtain flow information
B: Can assess flow in all branches of the intracerebral vessels
C: Provides real time flow evaluation
D: Can be performed during the surgical procedure
column2:
Answer: A. Technique does not require vessel puncture to obtain flow information

Feedback:
Angiography is an invasive procedure that requires a vessel puncture to administer contrast. Intraoperative US is performed by placing the transducer on the artery, no puncture required to obtain flow information. Both ultrasound and angiography can provide real time blood flow information because the contrast flow is evaluated live with fluoroscopy on angiography. Ultrasound does not evaluate all branches of the intracerebral vessels while angiography can visualize all patent branches in the FOV. Both techniques can be performed during the surgery.

column1:
When performing serial exams on an in situ venous bypass graft, _________________ indicates significant graft failure.

A: conversion of the waveform from triphasic to biphasic
B: a decrease in ABI of 0.10 from the last exam
C: an increase in end diastolic velocity greater than 30cm/s from the last exam
D: a decrease in peak systolic velocity of 30cm/s from the last exam
column2:
Answer: D. a decrease in peak systolic velocity of 30cm/s from the last exam

Feedback:
Serial exams that demonstrate a decrease in the PSV or 30cm/s or more and/or a decrease in the ABI of 0.15 or more indicate poor performance of the graft on the current exam.

column1:
A clot in the gastrocnemius veins will first propagate into what vessel(s)?

A: popliteal vein
B: anterior tibial veins
C: peroneal veins
D: deep femoral vein
column2:
Answer: A. popliteal vein

Feedback:
When answering questions regarding clot propagation, consider what vessel is proximal to the clot. When answering questions regarding distal flow changes due to a proximal clot, consider what vessel is emptying blood into the vessel with the clot. The gastrocnemius veins empty their blood into the popliteal vein in most patients. Gastrocnemius thrombosis will first extend into popliteal vein.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes a reversed vein graft?

A: stenosis at the proximal anastomosis is a common complication
B: the patient must wait at least 48 hrs after the valvulotome procedure is performed to complete the connection of the graft connection
C: commonly used to correct bilateral iliac stenosis
D: there is less scarring with a reversed vein graft procedure than an in situ graft procedure
column2:
Answer: A. stenosis at the proximal anastomosis is a common complication

Feedback:
The GSV is removed from the leg and replaced in a reversed position. The smaller distal end of the GSV is attached proximal to the obstruction in the femoral or popliteal vein. For this reason, stenosis of the proximal anastomosis is more common than other complications. The larger proximal end of the GSV is connected distal to the obstruction. Because the GSV is completely removed from the leg, there is more scarring than with an in-situ graft where the GSV remains in its natural position. The reversed vein graft is turned upside down, so the valve stays open due to gravity (no valvulotomy performed).

column1:
When a patient performs exercise on a treadmill what will be the normal change in the ankle brachial indices?

A: decreased by less than 0.3
B: increased by more than 0.3
C: unchanged or mild decrease
D: unchanged or mild increase
column2:
Answer: D. unchanged or mild increase

Feedback:
The pressure in the ankles and arms may demonstrate a mild increase in pressure with exercise but the brachial and ankle pressure will increase together in a normal patient. This will lead to a consistent ABI calculation. A mild increase in the ABI may also be normal. A decrease in ankle pressure indicates an abnormal response.

column1:
Which of the following describes symptoms of a patient with lymphedema?

A: Bilateral calf swelling with severe pitting edema
B: Unilateral left leg swelling that is relieved by elevating the legs for 30 minutes
C: Warm, red calf with significant swelling
D: Unilateral non-pitting edema that is unaffected by leg elevation
column2:
Answer: D. Unilateral non-pitting edema that is unaffected by leg elevation

Feedback:
Lymphedema does not normally cause skin discoloration, while acute DVT can be associated with warmth and redness. Lymphedema causes non-pitting edema, DVT causes pitting edema. Elevating the leg will have no effect on the swelling but can reduce swelling associated with DVT. Lymphedema is typically unilateral. DVT formation can affect one or both legs.

column1:
How do you adjust your technique to reduce or eliminate cross talk on a Doppler tracing?

A: Increase the PRF
B: Turn down the Doppler gain
C: Use the same approach and incident angle but use a CW non-imaging probe
D: Use a smaller sample size
column2:
Answer: B. Turn down the Doppler gain

Feedback:
The mirror image artifact on Doppler is caused by sampling close to a very reflective structure (bone) and by using a Doppler angle near 90 degrees. If you use the same approach but switch to CW, the same issue will occur. To fix the issue, try a new window that offers more space between the reflective structure and the artery, turn down the Doppler gain, or try a new window where the vessel course is less perpendicular to the probe.

column1:
A patient presents for a carotid ultrasound. The exam demonstrates significant calcification in the proximal ICA on the left side and the Doppler signal is not obtained. Antegrade flow in the mid ICA has a peak velocity of 30cm/s and end diastolic velocity of 10 cm/s. How will you report these findings?

A: Critical stenosis proximal left ICA.
B: Suspected critical stenosis in the proximal left ICA, but the exam was limited by shadowing from atheroma formation.
C: Normal exam.
D: Normal exam with limited evaluation of the proximal ICA.
column2:
Answer: B. Suspected critical stenosis in the proximal left ICA, but the exam was limited by shadowing from atheroma formation.

Feedback:
Any limitation in the exam that obscures your view of anatomy should be reported to the physician in the technologist findings.

column1:
What blood vessels are located at letter C?

A: 1 PTA and 2 PTV
B: 1 ATA and 2 ATV
C: 2 peroneal arteries and 1 peroneal vein
D: 2 peroneal veins and 1 peroneal artery
column2:
Answer: A. 1 PTA and 2 PTV

Feedback:

column1:
Hypothenar hammer syndrome and thoracic outlet syndrome are what type of vascular disorder?

A: Fibromuscular syndromes
B: Compression syndromes
C: Atherosclerotic diseases
D: Autoimmune diseases
column2:
Answer: B. Compression syndromes

Feedback:
TOS involves compression of the vessels in the thoracic outlet. Hypothenar hammer syndrome involves compression of the ulnar artery. Eagle syndrome involves compression of the carotid artery. SMA compression syndrome can affect the left renal vein or the duodenum. Median arcuate ligament syndrome involves compression of the celiac artery. There are many issues that can be caused by anatomic impingement of adjacent structures.

column1:
According to the Appropriate Use Criteria, which of the following is an appropriate indication for a screening ankle-brachial index exam?

A: Nocturnal pain or cramping with normal pulses on clinical exam
B: Patient with low Framingham risk score
C: 58yr old male with diabetes
D: Diabetic neuropathy with normal pulses
column2:
Answer: C. 58yr old male with diabetes

Feedback:
Dedicated ABI exams are recommended as a screening tool in patients with a femoral bruit or diminished pulses, patients >50yrs with diabetes or smoking history and all patients over 70yrs of age..

column1:
Which of the following will increase the amplitude of a digital PPG tracing?

A: adding more gel between the skin and the sensor
B: adjust the baseline of the tracing
C: warming the patient's foot and toes
D: increase sweep speed of the recorder
column2:
Answer: C. warming the patient's foot and toes

Feedback:
PPG exams should be performed in a warm room. The digits can be warmed to cause vasodilation and increase pulse amplitude. Because PPG measures blood volume, the amplitude increases with vasodilation and increased blood volume. A towel can be used to cover the digits and sensors to warm the digits and reduce the effects of surrounding light. The sweep speed affects the display of volume changes over time, not amplitude of the tracing. The baseline is used to indicate positive or negative direction of flow. Changes in baseline are used to highlight flow patterns toward or away from the transducer.

column1:
The 10cm blood pressure cuffs are missing from the lab. You perform the 3-cuff method lower extremity segmental pressure exam with the 12cm and 14cm cuffs on a patient that is 5'5" 150lbs. What information that you will report will be invalid?

A: brachial pressure, calf pressure and ankle pressure
B: thigh pressure only
C: brachial pressure only
D: calf pressure only
column2:
Answer: A. brachial pressure, calf pressure and ankle pressure

Feedback:
The 12cm or larger cuff is normally used for the thigh. If the larger cuff is used, the pressure will be underestimated in the calf, ankle and arm.

column1:
The image displayed is from the PTA from an arterial Duplex exam with normal ABIs and minimal atherosclerosis. Which of the following explains the findings?

A: The waveform is normal and is consistent with the other findings of the exam.
B: The Doppler gain is too low and pertinent information is not displayed.
C: Medial calcification is causing increased resistance to the flow causing a peaked appearance of the monophasic waveform.
D: The wall filter is set too high and is removing pertinent information from the display.
column2:
Answer: D. The wall filter is set too high and is removing pertinent information from the display.

Feedback:
Anytime a peaked monophasic waveform is displayed, adjustments to the machine settings should be made to better evaluate the flow for the diastolic flow components. Decrease the scale, adjust the baseline, decrease the rejection level or filter and readjust probe.

column1:
A patient presents for a 3-month follow-up on his aortic endograft. The aortic sac has increased in size by 0.8cm since the last ultrasound evaluation performed 2 months ago. No endoleak was detected on ultrasound or CT evaluation. What is the most likely cause for the diameter change?

A: Graft migration
B: Reactive hyperemia
C: Endotension
D: May Thurner syndrome
column2:
Answer: C. Endotension

Feedback:
Endotension- no leak present but there is continued expansion of the aneurysm sac greater than 5 mm; sometimes referred to as Type V Endoleak

column1:
The ______________ course(s) between the tibial and fibular head to empty blood into the popliteal vein.

A: peroneal veins
B: small saphenous vein
C: anterior tibial veins
D: posterior tibial veins
column2:
Answer: C. anterior tibial veins

Feedback:
ATVs course anterolaterally to the tibial bone across the interosseous membrane of the calf and through a space between the tibial and fibular head to empty into the popliteal vein.

column1:
Venous PPG tracings are obtained. The right side demonstrates a venous refill time of 30 seconds. The left side demonstrates a refill time of 23 seconds. Which of the following describes the next step in evaluation?

A: A tourniquet will be applied to both legs at the thigh level and the VRT assessment will be repeated.
B: A tourniquet will be applied to the left leg at the thigh level and the VRT assessment will be repeated.
C: A tourniquet will be applied to the right leg at the thigh level and the VRT assessment will be repeated.
D: The results are normal. No further evaluation is necessary.
column2:
Answer: D. The results are normal. No further evaluation is necessary.

Feedback:
A normal VRT is greater than 20 seconds.

column1:
Which of the following describes the best technique used to visualize and locate the inferior mesenteric artery?

A: coronal approach on the left side mid flank
B: sagittal approach to the right of midline near umbilicus
C: transverse approach, locate renal arteries and slide inferiorly
D: sagittal approach to the left of midline near umbilicus
column2:
Answer: C. transverse approach, locate renal arteries and slide inferiorly

Feedback:
The best technique used to visualize and locate the inferior mesenteric artery is to use a transverse approach to evaluate the aorta. After locating the renal artery origins, slide inferiorly to look for the IMA. The IMA originates from the anterior aorta just distal to the renal artery origins.

column1:
If the image displayed is a transverse view of the posterior right lower leg, Letter D correspond to:

A: popliteal vein
B: lateral gastrocnemius veins
C: medial gastrocnemius veins
D: posterior tibial veins
column2:
Answer: A. popliteal vein

Feedback:
When imaging the posterior leg, the notch on the transducer should be placed toward the right side of the patient, just like imaging the anterior leg. The right side of the image is lateral and the left side of the image is medial in both imaging views. A. small saphenous vein B. lateral gastrocnemius veins C. medial gastrocnemius veins D. popliteal vein

column1:
You are performing an upper extremity segmental pressure evaluation and obtain the following information: Brachial 140mmHg Upper forearm 132mmHg Wrist - Radial 108mmHg Wrist - Ulnar 130mmHg Which of the following correctly describes the findings?

A: subclavian steal
B: obstruction of the mid radial artery
C: obstruction of the mid ulnar artery
D: obstruction of the distal brachial artery
column2:
Answer: B. obstruction of the mid radial artery

Feedback:
The brachial and upper forearm pressure is normal, but there is a 22mmHg difference between the radial and ulnar pressures. The vessel with the lower pressure has an obstruction proximal to the wrist.

column1:
Which of the following Doppler information is necessary to calculate the resistive index?

A: peak systolic velocity and peak diastolic velocity
B: peak systolic velocity, average flow velocity and end diastolic velocity
C: peak systolic velocity and end diastolic velocity
D: peak systolic velocity before and after a stenosis
column2:
Answer: C. peak systolic velocity and end diastolic velocity

Feedback:
The resistive index = PSV - EDV / PSV

column1:
Normal brachial pressures should not differ more than ____________ mmHg between arms.

A: 5
B: 10
C: 30
D: 20
column2:
Answer: D. 20

Feedback:
If the brachial pressures differ by more than 20mmHg, a subclavian steal should be suspected.

column1:
The posterior communicating artery connects the:

A: ECA to the ICA
B: anterior and posterior cerebral vessels
C: right and left sides of the circle of willis
D: right and left vertebral arteries
column2:
Answer: B. anterior and posterior cerebral vessels

Feedback:
The posterior communicators connect the posterior cerebral circulation to the anterior cerebral circulation (PCA and the MCA). The ACA and MCA are part of the anterior circulation. The PCA and basilar/vertebral arteries are part of the posterior circulation.

column1:
What two vessels are palpated to assess the pulse when peripheral arterial disease is suspected?

A: dorsalis pedis and peroneal arteries
B: anterior tibial and peroneal arteries
C: posterior tibial and dorsalis pedis arteries
D: posterior tibial and peroneal arteries
column2:
Answer: C. posterior tibial and dorsalis pedis arteries

Feedback:
If peripheral arterial disease is suspected, the physician will palpate the PTA and DPA to assess the strength of the pulse. The PTA is palpated between the posterior border of the medial malleolus and the Achilles tendon. The DPA is palpated at the dorsal most prominence of the navicular bone. The peroneal artery is not usually able to be palpated due to its anatomic course.

column1:
Which of the following is important information to record on the final report for a carotid Doppler exam on a patient with 50-69% ICA stenosis?

A: Mechanical index settings
B: Doppler angles used on the exam
C: Transducer frequency used for the exam
D: Recommendation for TCD exam for associated intracerebral disease
column2:
Answer: B. Doppler angles used on the exam

Feedback:
A patient with 50-69% stenosis will be followed with serial ultrasound examinations annually or semiannually. The Doppler angles used in this exam should be duplicated on the serial exams for the most accurate assessment of changes in the level of stenosis.

column1:
When continuous TCD monitoring is used to assist with a carotid endarterectomy, when is the TCD device first activated to begin monitoring the patient?

A: immediately after the endarterectomy was performed but before the wound closure is performed
B: within 24hrs after the endarterectomy was performed
C: just before the surgery starts
D: within 1-2 hours after the endarterectomy was performed
column2:
Answer: C. just before the surgery starts

Feedback:
The patient's intracranial blood flow is monitored closely to identify and count any embolic signatures that may occur during the endarterectomy, and closing phase of the surgery, as well as in the recovery room.

column1:
Which renal arteries are evaluated with Doppler in an ultrasound exam that is used to perform a direct evaluation for renal artery stenosis?

A: main renal artery
B: main renal artery, segmental and arcuate arteries
C: aorta
D: segmental and arcuate arteries
column2:
Answer: A. main renal artery

Feedback:
A direct evaluation uses Doppler to evaluate the velocity in the main renal artery segments. The indirect evaluation uses Doppler to evaluate the flow in the segmental/parenchymal arteries. The RI and AT values are assessed and the shape of the waveform is considered in the diagnosis. (not peak velocity) The indirect evaluation looks for signs of stenosis in the main renal artery by looking at the flow in the organ distally.

column1:
Flow through venous perforators is:

A: normally unidirectional from the superficial to the deep venous system
B: normally unidirectional from the deep venous to the superficial system
C: normally bidirectional to help equalize pressures in the deep and superficial systems
D: normally absent unless the deep system becomes overloaded and forces the excess blood into the superficial system
column2:
Answer: A. normally unidirectional from the superficial to the deep venous system

Feedback:
Flow through venous perforators is normally unidirectional from the superficial to the deep venous system.

column1:
A patient presents with gangrene of the foot and toes. The most likely cause of these symptoms is:

A: coarctation of the aorta
B: extensive deep and superficial vein thrombosis
C: chronic occlusion of the popliteal artery
D: a 5cm aortic aneurysm with mural thrombus
column2:
Answer: B. extensive deep and superficial vein thrombosis

Feedback:
Venous gangrene usually involves the foot and all toes, while arterial gangrene usually involves one or two toes.

column1:
What is the most accurate method of thigh pressure assessment?

A: 3-cuff segmental pressure exam
B: Intravenous catheter measures pressure in the superficial femoral vein
C: 4-cuff segmental pressure exam
D: Doppler velocity used in the Bernoulli equation to calculate thigh pressure
column2:
Answer: A. 3-cuff segmental pressure exam

Feedback:
The 4-cuff method uses two smaller cuffs to obtain two separate thigh pressures. These cuffs are usually smaller than the thigh cuff used in the 3-cuff method. Smaller cuffs tend to overestimate the thigh pressure. The larger cuff used in the 3- cuff method provides a more accurate pressure.

column1:
Which of the following causes flow reversal in venous perforators?

A: superficial thrombosis
B: deep vein thrombosis
C: compression stockings
D: combination of superficial and deep thrombosis
column2:
Answer: B. deep vein thrombosis

Feedback:
DVT in the deep system forces any remaining flow into areas of lower pressure, the perforators. Flow exits the deep system through the perforators to try to "escape" into the patent superficial system.

column1:
Which of the following describes an AV malformation?

A: can be treated using Thrombin injection
B: dilated venous collaterals adjacent to the area of interest
C: direct connection between a single artery and vein
D: caused by trauma or surgical procedures
column2:
Answer: B. dilated venous collaterals adjacent to the area of interest

Feedback:
An AVM is a congenital malformation with multiple connections between the arterioles and venules without a capillary bed in between. Venous collaterals will develop and dilate over time. An AVF is an acquired condition that is caused by trauma or invasive procedures. Because it is an acute complication, no collateral vessels form. The arterial inflow and venous outflow will demonstrate the same hemodynamic characteristics for an AVM and an AVF, low resistance arterial inflow and pulsatile, turbulent venous outflow. Thrombin cannot be used to treat an AVM or AVF because it could occlude the arterial system.

column1:
An abnormal Penile/Brachial Index will be:

A: < 1.0
B: > 1.0
C: < 0.65
D: > 0.65
column2:
Answer: C. < 0.65

Feedback:
If the penile blood pressure is less than 65% of the systemic pressure, vasculogenic impotence is diagnosed. A normal PBI is greater than or equal to 0.75. If the PBI is between 0.65 and 0.75, it is considered only a marginal reduction and vasculogenic cause for erectile dysfunction cannot be diagnosed.

column1:
Which of the following causes of thrombosis is associated with anatomic variations of the thoracic outlet?

A: indwelling catheters
B: radiation therapy treatments to the chest
C: shoulder surgery
D: Paget - Schroetter Syndrome
column2:
Answer: D. Paget - Schroetter Syndrome

Feedback:
Paget - Schroetter Syndrome AKA spontaneous effort thrombosis: Most common cause of axillary/subclavian DVT in the ambulatory patient population Related to the presence of a cervical rib and thoracic outlet syndrome Higher incidence in the dominant arm Indwelling catheters are a common cause of upper extremity thrombosis in patients that are ill and require intravenous medication/treatment

column1:
An AV malformation is most commonly caused by:

A: trauma
B: recent angiography procedure
C: hemodialysis access
D: a congenital defect
column2:
Answer: D. a congenital defect

Feedback:
An AVM is a congenital defect. An AVF is caused by trauma or an interventional procedure.

column1: Use your mouse to place your cursor over the superior mesenteric artery and click to mark the vessel. If the vessel is not demonstrated on the image, mark the purple box that says "Not demonstrated on the image".
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
The SMA is identified anterior to the aorta and posterior to the body of the pancreas. The left renal vein courses between the aorta and the SMA to join the IVC.

column1:
The PW Doppler tracing was obtained from the CCA. Which statement below is true regarding the image?

A: Normal high resistance, triphasic flow is demonstrated
B: Abnormal high resistance flow due to increased flow in Vertebral supply
C: Abnormal high resistance flow due to ICA occlusion
D: Abnormal high resistance flow due to proximal stenosis
column2:
Answer: C. Abnormal high resistance flow due to ICA occlusion

Feedback:
Note the flow reversal in diastole with no antegrade flow during the full diastolic portion of the cycle. This indicates significant resistance distal to the area evaluated. An occluded ICA can lead to the increase in resistance in the CCA which causes the waveform type displayed on the image.

column1:
If the acceleration time is >140ms in both common femoral arteries:

A: unilateral external iliac disease is suspected
B: flow is considered normal.
C: aortic disease is suspected
D: bilateral internal iliac disease is suspected
column2:
Answer: C. aortic disease is suspected

Feedback:
If the acceleration time is >140ms in both common femoral arteries, aortic disease is suspected. Increased or longer AT indicates a proximal obstruction. If the acceleration time is >140ms in one common femoral artery, ipsilateral iliac stenosis is suspected. Decreased or shorter AT indicates a distal obstruction.

column1:
Anticoagulant therapy is a contraindication for:

A: cold immersion testing
B: a transcranial duplex exam
C: ultrasound guided thrombin injection
D: a segmental pressure exam
column2:
Answer: C. ultrasound guided thrombin injection

Feedback:
Anticoagulant therapy is a contraindication for pseudoaneurysm treatment. The goal of compression therapy and thrombin injection is to promote thrombus formation. Anticoagulant therapy works to reduce thrombus formation.

column1:
Which of the following conditions is usually related to cardiac pulsatility in the leg veins?

A: Bradycardia
B: Tachycardia
C: DVT
D: Congestive heart failure
column2:
Answer: D. Congestive heart failure

Feedback:
As the heart moves in the chest, it causes the ripple effect in the venous system. Normally, the upper IVC and Hepatic veins demonstrate this ripple effect as flow reversal during cardiac systole. The "ripples" of cardiac pressure/motion usually dissipate as they reach the mid abdomen area. In patients with CHF, the venous blood volume is increased throughout the system. Cardiac pulsatility will extend further into the lower extremities due to the increased blood volume.

column1:
A patient presents for an upper extremity venous Doppler evaluation due to bilateral arm and face swelling. There are also varicosities visible on the upper chest wall. These symptoms are most consistent with:

A: occlusive thrombus in the superior vena cava
B: occlusive thrombus in the internal jugular vein
C: congestive heart failure and venous stasis
D: Nutcracker syndrome
column2:
Answer: A. occlusive thrombus in the superior vena cava

Feedback:
Chest wall varices, facial and bilateral arm swelling are indicative of SVC thrombosis. IJV thrombosis can be related to facial swelling, but the arm would not be affected. CHF usually causes swelling in the legs.

column1:
Which of the following describes a cardiovascular application of the Bernoulli Principle?

A: explains the reason for aliasing of the Doppler signal at high velocities
B: explains the reason for increased resistance to flow at an area of focal stenosis
C: explains the relationship of vessel radius and flow volume
D: explains the turbulence and layers of flow separation within the carotid bulb
column2:
Answer: D. explains the turbulence and layers of flow separation within the carotid bulb

Feedback:
Bernoulli Principle explains the turbulence and layers of flow separation within the carotid bulb.

column1:
Which of the following is related to chronic DVT?

A: Engorged, non-compressible vein
B: Dry ulceration
C: Swelling and erythema
D: Wet ulceration
column2:
Answer: D. Wet ulceration

Feedback:
Ulcerations take time to form. They are a sign of chronic venous disease. Venous ulcers are wet/oozy and mildly painful. Arterial ulcers more painful and dry.

column1:
Assuming proper transducer orientation, which of the following normal vessels could be displayed on the image?

A: superior vena cava and right innominate vein
B: left common femoral vein and greater saphenous vein
C: left subclavian vein and cephalic vein
D: right axillary vein and cephalic vein
column2:
Answer: D. right axillary vein and cephalic vein

Feedback:
The flow in the blue vessel is moving into the red vessel. The flow in the red vessel is moving toward the right side of the image. This is a transverse image of the right shoulder. The left side of the image is the lateral arm. The right side of the image is the medial arm. The vessels would be reversed on the image of this was a left shoulder image. If this were a longitudinal view of the groin it would demonstrate the greater saphenous vein extending upward on the left side of the image.

column1:
Which of the following indicates a balloon angioplasty of an 80% stenosis of the superficial femoral artery was a success?

A: velocity ratio greater than 2.0
B: increased damping in the inflow artery
C: decrease in the pulsatility index distal to the treated segment
D: an increase in the ABI of at least 0.15
column2:
Answer: D. an increase in the ABI of at least 0.15

Feedback:
Resolution of patient symptoms, an ABI increase >0.15 and <50% diameter stenosis on duplex ultrasound indicate a successful PTLA. Angioplasty failure = damped, low-velocity waveforms in the distal arterial tree, no change in the post-procedure ABI.

column1:
Which of the following is a contraindication for harvesting of the great saphenous vein?

A: valvular incompetence in the great saphenous vein
B: chronic obstruction of the peroneal veins
C: chronic obstruction of the femoral vein
D: Baker's cyst in the ipsilateral popliteal fossa
column2:
Answer: C. chronic obstruction of the femoral vein

Feedback:
IT IS VERY IMPORTANT to evaluate the venous outflow system of a patient prior to removal or closure of the saphenous vein. If the deep system is obstructed and the superficial system is the main outflow for the leg, the saphenous vein cannot be removed/closed.

column1:
Respiration and ____________________ are responsible for venous blood flow.

A: vessel recoil
B: Valsalva maneuvers
C: the calf muscle pump
D: the tunica media layer of the wall
column2:
Answer: C. the calf muscle pump

Feedback:
Respiration and the calf muscle pump are responsible for moving venous blood through the body.

column1:
If there is a 90% stenosis in the left carotid siphon, which of the following is true?

A: left vertebral flow will be high resistance
B: left MCA flow will be high resistance
C: left mid ICA flow will be high resistance
D: right MCA will be high resistance
column2:
Answer: C. left mid ICA flow will be high resistance

Feedback:
Distal stenosis will lead to high resistance flow proximally = ICA high resistance with siphon stenosis Proximal stenosis will lead to low resistance flow distally = MCA and ACA lower resistance with siphon stenosis

column1:
A segmental pressure exam demonstrates the following pressures: right arm: 136mmHg right leg: thigh 110mmHg, calf 100mmHg, ankle 92mmHg. Which of the following could explain these results?

A: aortic disease or popliteal disease
B: common iliac disease or distal femoral artery
C: medial calcification
D: aortic disease or common iliac disease
column2:
Answer: D. aortic disease or common iliac disease

Feedback:
The pressures in the entire leg are decreased which indicates proximal disease. Using the 3-cuff method, the brachial pressure and thigh pressure are usually relatively the same. If the left leg is affected also, aortic disease is the most likely diagnosis. If only the right leg is affected, then the cause is related to common iliac disease.

column1:
When Thrombin is injected in a pseudoaneurysm, the clotting should be begin:

A: after 1 minute of manual compression following the injection
B: within 5-10 minutes post- injection
C: within 5-10 seconds post- injection
D: within 24 hours
column2:
Answer: C. within 5-10 seconds post- injection

Feedback:
Thrombin administration is performed by injecting the clotting agent into a pseudoaneurysm to close the opening and clot the blood that has escaped. Clotting should occur immediately after injection (5-10 seconds). About 20 minutes after the procedure, a follow up exam should be performed to document the pseudoaneurysm is fully thrombosed.

column1:
Which of the waveforms demonstrates antegrade flow in diastole?

A: C only
B: B only
C: A, C, D
D: A, B, D
column2:
Answer: C. A, C, D

Feedback:
A. monophasic, high resistance B. biphasic C. Triphasic D. monophasic, low resistance Monophasic arterial flow indicates forward flow throughout the cardiac cycle. Biphasic arterial flow has two components of flow, antegrade flow in systole and retrograde flow in diastole. Triphasic arterial flow has three components of flow, antegrade flow in systole, retrograde flow in diastole, and a small segment of antegrade flow at the end of diastole.

column1:
The left and right innominate veins merge to form the ____________________.

A: Brachiocephalic vein
B: inferior vena cava (IVC)
C: superior vena cava (SVC)
D: Right atrium
column2:
Answer: C. superior vena cava (SVC)

Feedback:
The right and left innominate veins merge to form the SVC.

column1:
Which of the following carries an increased risk of developing varicose veins?

A: Sitting with legs elevated for long periods of time
B: Wearing compression stockings
C: Wearing flat shoes instead of high heels
D: Oral contraceptive use
column2:
Answer: D. Oral contraceptive use

Feedback:
Oral contraceptive use will increase the risk of developing varicose veins. Family history, pregnancy and obesity also increase the risk. The other three choices would reduce the risk of developing varicose veins.

column1:
Which of the questions listed below would provide pertinent history information for a lower extremity venous Doppler exam?

A: Do your legs/calves hurt more when walking, but the pain stops if you sit and rest?
B: If you on the edge of the bed with your feet on the floor, does this reduce the swelling?
C: Does your job require you to stand for long periods of time?
D: Do you have any ulcer formation on your toes?
column2:
Answer: C. Does your job require you to stand for long periods of time?

Feedback:
Standing for long periods of time can cause venous insufficiency and the related symptoms in this patient. Arterial disease can cause leg pain with exercise that is relieved by rest. The muscles require an increased blood supply with exercise. If there is arterial disease present, it may not allow the necessary increase in flow and ischemia/pain presents. Stopping the exercise will assist in the relief of the symptoms as the muscles decrease their need for blood flow. Arterial ulcers are found on the toes and heel of the foot. Venous ulcers are found on the distal calves.

column1:
Which of the following correctly explains why a surgeon would perform an intravascular ultrasound immediately after stent placement in small artery?

A: To assess the velocity of flow in the artery
B: To evaluate the stent position
C: To confirm that all plaque was removed from the artery
D: To assess the velocity of flow in the stent
column2:
Answer: B. To evaluate the stent position

Feedback:
Intravascular ultrasound uses a 10-30MHz transducer. IVUS allows for 360-degree imaging of the artery lumen. It is used to locate the narrowest segment that would benefit from stent placement, used after angiography to evaluate proper stent placement, used to evaluate coronary artery disease from within the coronary arteries. The transducers allow 2D imaging only, NO Doppler capabilities

column1:
Which of the following originates first from the ascending aorta?

A: coronary arteries
B: subclavian artery
C: left carotid artery
D: innominate artery
column2:
Answer: A. coronary arteries

Feedback:
Keep in mind that the question asks for the first branch of the ascending aorta, not the arch. The coronary arteries originate just above the aortic valve in the heart.

column1:
If there is a 75% stenosis of the distal axillary artery, which of the following arteries will demonstrate a triphasic waveform?

A: ulnar
B: subclavian
C: brachial
D: radial
column2:
Answer: B. subclavian

Feedback:
A 75% stenosis in the axillary artery will cause a significant loss of flow distally. The resistance in the distal vessels will drop and the waveforms will become monophasic. The subclavian artery is proximal to the stenosis and will remain high resistance.

column1:
Which of the following statements is true regarding Doppler flow evaluation of a rejected renal allograft?

A: Rejected allograft waveforms will demonstrate high resistance flow.
B: Rejected allograft waveforms will demonstrate low resistance flow.
C: Rejected allograft waveforms will demonstrate low RI and PI values.
D: Renal allograft waveforms normally demonstrate arterial flow that varies with respiration.
column2:
Answer: A. Rejected allograft waveforms will demonstrate high resistance flow.

Feedback:
Doppler waveforms from arteries in a rejected allograft will demonstrate high resistance flow. The RI values for the parenchymal vessels will be elevated above 0.70.

column1:
The transducer is placed over the mid femoral vein while calf compression is performed. The Doppler waveform demonstrates spontaneous flow with an increase in forward flow with the calf compression. Which of the following describes the findings?

A: The flow response is normal for the femoral vein.
B: Heart disease is most likely present which causes the abnormal flow response.
C: The popliteal and distal femoral veins have incompetent valves.
D: The popliteal vein has incompetent valves.
column2:
Answer: A. The flow response is normal for the femoral vein.

Feedback:
A normal response to distal augmentation is an increase in forward flow with the calf compression and NO flow reversal after the increase in flow.

column1:
A __________ cuff is usually used to assess wrist pressure and a ____________ cuff is usually used to assess digit pressure.

A: 10cm, 7cm
B: 7cm, 2.5cm
C: 2.5cm, 1cm
D: 12cm, 2.5cm
column2:
Answer: B. 7cm, 2.5cm

Feedback:
A 7cm cuff is usually used to assess wrist pressure and a 2.5cm cuff is usually used to assess digit pressure. Cuff size should be 20% larger than the diameter of the limb. The wrist is larger than the digits in diameter = larger cuff for wrist.

column1:
During a color evaluation of the common femoral vein, the patient is asked to perform the Valsalva maneuver. The blue color in the CFV changes to red for approximately 3 seconds before returning to blue. What do these color Doppler findings indicate?

A: The CFV has minimal venous insufficiency.
B: Superficial venous insufficiency is present.
C: There is most likely an occlusive DVT in the superficial femoral vein causing the venous turbulence proximally.
D: A significant level of insufficiency is present and should be further evaluated with PW or CW Doppler techniques.
column2:
Answer: D. A significant level of insufficiency is present and should be further evaluated with PW or CW Doppler techniques.

Feedback:
0.5 seconds or less of flow reversal is considered normal in the superficial veins. 1 sec or less of flow reversal is considered normal in the deep veins. 3 seconds or greater of flow reversal indicates significant venous reflux.

column1:
Which of the following is NOT normally a part of a test for vasculogenic impotence?

A: Obtain Doppler samples of the CFA, PTA and DPA
B: Obtain blood pressures in the arms and ankles
C: Measure the length of the cavernous arteries
D: Obtain Doppler samples of the cavernous arteries and veins in the penis
column2:
Answer: C. Measure the length of the cavernous arteries

Feedback:
When performing a penile Doppler exam, obtain Doppler samples of the CFA, cavernous arteries and veins in the penis. Doppler samples will also be taken in the PTA and DPA while performing ankle pressures. The ABI should be evaluated to determine if PAD can be causing the erectile dysfunction. The diameter of the cavernous arteries should be measured.

column1:
What causes the 3rd phase of flow in a normal extremity arterial waveform?

A: relaxation of the extremity musculature
B: arterial wall compliance
C: cardiac systole
D: relaxation of the left ventricle
column2:
Answer: B. arterial wall compliance

Feedback:

column1:
Which of the following requires the use of sterile technique?

A: Laser ablation for telangiectasia
B: Vein mapping
C: Radiofrequency venous ablation
D: Sclerotherapy
column2:
Answer: C. Radiofrequency venous ablation

Feedback:
Radiofrequency ablation requires the sterile technique because the procedure requires the puncture of the saphenous vein to insert a catheter into the length of the vein. The other choices do not require the sterile technique. Telangiectasia refers to dilated very small superficial veins that are treated with external application of laser treatment. Sclerotherapy refers to the injection of a solution into spider veins to cause them to atrophy and requires aseptic technique. Vein mapping is performed with ultrasound.

column1:
Overgaining on an US exam can cause ________________________.

A: underestimation of arterial stenosis
B: overestimation of Doppler velocity
C: increased posterior shadowing
D: blood to appear anechoic
column2:
Answer: B. overestimation of Doppler velocity

Feedback:
Overgaining on a Doppler exam will cause a false increase is the peak velocity of the flow.

column1:
This patient presents with a bruit, palpable mass, mild SOB and dizziness. These findings are most suggestive of:

A: AV fistula
B: Pseudoaneurysm
C: Carotid body tumor
D: Thyroid mass
column2:
Answer: C. Carotid body tumor

Feedback:
With only a 2D image, a diagnosis regarding the presence or absence of stenosis cannot be accurately made. The arrow indicates a large mass at the CCA bifurcation that surrounds the ICA. This is the classic appearance and location for a carotid body tumor. The symptoms listed are commonly seen with this type of tumor. The thyroid is located much more distally in the neck, therefore a thyroid mass is very unlikely.

column1:
While scanning the popliteal artery, you notice a thrombus in the popliteal vein. Which vein(s) should be evaluated next for ascending propagation of the clot?

A: common femoral vein
B: GSV
C: distal femoral vein
D: ATA and tibioperoneal trunk
column2:
Answer: C. distal femoral vein

Feedback:
If a clot is identified in the popliteal vein, the distal femoral vein should be evaluated next for propagation of the thrombus.

column1:
A patient complains of left arm numbness and pain that increases in severity when it is cold outside. An upper extremity arterial duplex exam demonstrates a>50% stenosis in the distal subclavian artery. The PPG exam demonstrates an additional decrease in digital flow with cold exposure. What explains these findings?

A: Thoracic Outlet Syndrome
B: Secondary Raynaud Phenomenon
C: Primary Raynaud Syndrome
D: Buerger Disease
column2:
Answer: B. Secondary Raynaud Phenomenon

Feedback:
Changes in flow patterns in the hand due to the application of external stimuli (cold) are a characteristic sign of Raynaud Disease. Secondary Raynaud Phenomenon refers to these changes that occur in conjunction with a diseased arterial system in the upper extremity. Primary Raynaud Syndrome refers to these changes that occur in conjunction with a non-diseased arterial system in the upper extremity.

column1: When measuring the AP diameter of the aortic aneurysm, where should the caliper be placed at the anterior wall? Use your mouse to position the cursor at the correct caliper location and click to set your cursor.
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
The AP measurement of the aorta is obtained where the US beam is most perpendicular to the vessel wall. The aorta should be measured from outer wall to outer wall.

column1:
Which vessel(s) supplies the majority of the blood to the hand?

A: metatarsal arteries
B: ulnar artery
C: radial artery
D: digital veins
column2:
Answer: B. ulnar artery

Feedback:
The ulnar artery supplies the majority of the blood to the hand.

column1:
A patient presents with localized swelling and redness of the left calf that started two days ago. The referring physician requested a segmental pressure exam. What should you do first?

A: Start with the brachial pressures and then perform the lower extremity pressures.
B: Discuss the order with the radiologist to confirm that the exam that is ordered should be performed.
C: Change the order to a venous Doppler after you speak to the patient regarding their symptoms.
D: Start with the ankle pressures, then move up the leg and finish with the brachial pressures.
column2:
Answer: B. Discuss the order with the radiologist to confirm that the exam that is ordered should be performed.

Feedback:
Anytime the procedure could be detrimental to the patient, you should clarify the order with the radiologist or referring physician. This patient demonstrates symptoms and signs of a possible acute DVT. Applying pressure cuffs to a vein with thrombus in it could cause emboli propagation.

column1:
What can be done to improve the visualization of the anterior aortic wall?

A: increase the compression
B: center the probe with the ultrasound beam perpendicular to the aortic wall
C: decrease the threshold setting.
D: scan from a more lateral approach
column2:
Answer: B. center the probe with the ultrasound beam perpendicular to the aortic wall

Feedback:
Perpendicular incidence of the beam to the reflector will increase specular reflection. Increasing the amount of specular reflection will improve the visibility of the anterior aortic wall. 2D imaging relies on perpendicular incidence of the beam to the structure while Doppler imaging relies on parallel incidence of the beam to the blood flow.

column1:
Which transducer should be selected for a renal artery Doppler examination for a patient that is 5'5" and 150lbs?

A: 2.5MHz curvilinear array
B: 3.5MHz curvilinear array
C: 3.5MHz phased array
D: 3.5MHz linear array
column2:
Answer: B. 3.5MHz curvilinear array

Feedback:
A curved transducer is necessary for abdominal vascular evaluations. 3.5MHz should be sufficient for an average sized patient.

column1:
Hemodynamically significant stenosis of the SMA is diagnosed when the peak systolic velocity is greater than?

A: 1.0 m/s
B: 1.75 m/s
C: 2.0 m/s
D: 2.75 m/s
column2:
Answer: D. 2.75 m/s

Feedback:
Hemodynamically significant stenosis of the SMA is diagnosed with the PSV greater than 2.75 m/s.

column1: Where should the second caliper be placed to measure the acceleration time of the renal artery waveform? Use your mouse to position the cursor and click to set your answer.
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
Normal renal artery waveforms demonstrate an early systolic peak. When measuring the AT, the first caliper is placed at the start of systole and the second caliper is placed at the top of the early systolic peak. When measuring the resistive index, the first caliper is placed at the true peak systolic velocity and the second caliper is placed at the peak end diastolic velocity.

column1:
Where do the plantar arteries originate?

A: from the peroneal artery just past the Achilles tendon attachment at the heel
B: from the anterior tibial artery anterior to the medial malleolus
C: from the anterior tibial artery posterior to the medial malleolus
D: from the posterior tibial artery posterior to the medial malleolus
column2:
Answer: D. from the posterior tibial artery posterior to the medial malleolus

Feedback:
The plantar arteries originate from the PTA posterior to the medial malleolus.

column1:
Letter B indicates which of the following structures?

A: Superficial temporal artery
B: Vertebral artery
C: Internal thoracic artery
D: Thyrocervical trunk
column2:
Answer: B. Vertebral artery

Feedback:
A. Internal thoracic artery B. Vertebral artery C. Thyrocervical trunk D. Right CCA

column1:
Which of the following conditions would cause an increase in the systemic BP in just the right arm?

A: thoracic outlet syndrome of the right arm
B: right subclavian artery stenosis
C: aortic coarctation just distal to the origin of the left common carotid artery
D: right subclavian steal
column2:
Answer: C. aortic coarctation just distal to the origin of the left common carotid artery

Feedback:
If aortic coarctation occurs just distal to the origin of the left common carotid artery, BP will be reduced in the left arm and increased in the right arm. BP will be reduced in the right arm with the other choices.

column1:
Restenosis of a carotid stent most commonly occurs:

A: At the stent attachment sites
B: In the proximal stent
C: In the distal stent
D: In the native artery distal to the stent
column2:
Answer: A. At the stent attachment sites

Feedback:
Restenosis of a carotid stent most commonly occurs at the attachment sites of the stent. The turbulent flow and vessel healing in these areas will cause plaque formation and the overproduction of normal cells.

column1:
If both common iliac arteries demonstrate significant stenosis, what type of treatment will most likely be used?

A: left axillary - femoral bypass graft
B: fem-fem bypass graft
C: right axillary - femoral bypass graft
D: kissing stents
column2:
Answer: D. kissing stents

Feedback:
Kissing stents are used for stenosis at the origin of the common iliac arteries. Two stents are deployed, one is placed into each iliac artery. Both stents are inflated with the catheter balloon. The proximal end of the stents are touching in the distal aorta (kissing).

column1:
What blood vessel is used to cause the variations in diastolic flow on the ECA tracing?

A: common carotid artery
B: supraorbital artery
C: superficial temporal artery
D: superficial thyroidal artery
column2:
Answer: C. superficial temporal artery

Feedback:
The temporal tap is performed by lightly tapping the temporal artery located at the soft spot on the anterior aspect of the temporal bone. The temporal artery is a branch of the ECA and the vibrations from the tapping will be seen within the ECA waveform. The ICA does not communicate with the temporal artery and no response will be identified from the tapping maneuver. This technique is used to differentiate the ECA from the ICA.

column1:
The images displayed are from a renal Doppler exam on a 15yr old female with significant HTN. What is the most likely cause for the abnormality demonstrated?

A: Suprarenal AAA
B: Infrarenal AAA
C: Fibromuscular dysplasia
D: Atherosclerotic disease
column2:
Answer: C. Fibromuscular dysplasia

Feedback:
The bilateral presentation of renal stenosis in a pediatric patient is most likely due to a diffuse disorder of congenital or idiopathic nature. Atherosclerosis formation is a chronic disease. Suprarenal AAA would reduce the blood flow to the kidneys increasing the BP but would not cause the increase in velocity in the arteries.

column1:
If the CCA PSV is 80cm/s and the CCA EDV is 40cm/s, what is the systolic velocity ratio?

A: 1.0
B: 2.1
C: 4.9
D: 9.7
column2:
Answer: C. 4.9

Feedback:
Systolic Velocity Ratio = PSV ICA / PSV CCA 388cm/s / 80 cm/s = 4.9 There should not be challenging calculations on the registry exam because you will not have access to a calculator. The importance of this question is to know how to make the calculation.

column1:
A critical ICA stenosis will have what effect on the ipsilateral middle cerebral artery flow?

A: decreased diastolic flow
B: no effect
C: increased diastolic flow
D: increased systolic flow
column2:
Answer: A. decreased diastolic flow

Feedback:
A waveform distal to a critical stenosis will demonstrate a blunted monophasic waveform with minimal or absent diastolic flow.

column1:
Flow distal to a significant stenosis will exhibit which of the following characteristics?

A: higher resistance flow
B: lower resistance flow
C: increased velocity
D: increased resistive index
column2:
Answer: B. lower resistance flow

Feedback:
Flow distal to a significant stenosis will demonstrate reduced velocity and resistance.

column1:
What is the name of the tiny network of vessels that supply/drain blood to/from the vessel wall?

A: arterioles
B: vasa vasorum
C: capillaries
D: venules
column2:
Answer: B. vasa vasorum

Feedback:
The tiny network of vessels that supply/drain blood to/from the vessel wall is called the vasa vasorum. The tiny vessels wrap around the larger vessel within the tunica externa. The arteries and veins penetrate the tunica media to deliver and drain blood from the wall.

column1:
The image provided was taken during a routine carotid examination. Which of the following statements is true regarding the images?

A: This is a normal vertebral artery
B: This is a normal ICA
C: This is a mildly stenosed ICA
D: This is a normal ECA
column2:
Answer: D. This is a normal ECA

Feedback:
Note the branch originating from the proximal vessel. The ICA has no branches identified extracranially. The vertebral arteries course through the pedicles of the vertebra and this vessel is demonstrated anterior to the spine.

column1:
A patient presents for a lower extremity venous Doppler exam due to a history of May-Thurner Syndrome. What vessel should you closely evaluate for related findings?

A: bilateral distal femoral veins
B: left iliac vein
C: right iliac vein
D: bilateral popliteal veins
column2:
Answer: B. left iliac vein

Feedback:
May - Thurner Syndrome refers to increased incidence of left DVT due to extrinsic compression by RIGHT iliac artery.

column1:
_________________________ carry(s) the blood from the GSV in the distal thigh into the distal femoral vein.

A: Lateral perforators
B: Paratibial perforators
C: Thigh perforators
D: Posterior venous arch
column2:
Answer: C. Thigh perforators

Feedback:
The distal thigh perforators, formerly known as Dodd's perforators, drain blood from the GSV in the distal thigh into the distal femoral vein.

column1:
What setting on the photoplethysmography system should remain constant during an extremity arterial evaluation and if it is adjusted it must be documented on the tracing?

A: Baseline level
B: Sweep speed
C: Size control
D: Signal output
column2:
Answer: C. Size control

Feedback:
The size control on the PPG system should be set at a constant level (10) and should not be changed during the exam. The PPG tracings demonstrate the amplitude of the reflected light, which indicates blood volume changes in the toe. The size setting should be kept constant to best demonstrate changes in blood volume between toes/feet. If the size control is adjusted, it must be documented on the affected tracings.

column1:
The image demonstrated is from a routine carotid ultrasound. Based on this image, what should be done next to evaluate this patient?

A: Evaluate the left subclavian artery for the presence of monophasic flow
B: Perform a TCD exam to look for a contralateral MCA for obstruction
C: Evaluate the right subclavian artery for the presence of monophasic flow
D: Evaluate the left vertebral to see if the waveform is also high resistance
column2:
Answer: D. Evaluate the left vertebral to see if the waveform is also high resistance

Feedback:
The high resistance triphasic waveform in the vertebral artery indicates a distal obstruction. If the left vertebral is also triphasic, there is most likely a basilar obstruction. If the left vertebral is normal and monophasic, there must be a distal vertebral obstruction on the right side. A TCD exam could identify an obstruction in the basilar artery that would explain this vertebral waveform.

column1:
Normal flow in the splenic artery:

A: is hepatopetal.
B: is high resistant.
C: demonstrates Reynold's number >2000.
D: is laminar.
column2:
Answer: C. demonstrates Reynold's number >2000.

Feedback:
The splenic artery is the most tortuous artery in the body and carries very turbulent flow. A Reynolds number over 2000 indicates the point where turbulence is identified on color Doppler.

column1:
If a renal artery Doppler exam demonstrates normal flow, but stenosis is identified on angiography evaluation, the US results are described as:

A: true positive
B: false positive
C: true negative
D: false negative
column2:
Answer: D. false negative

Feedback:
If the current exam is negative but the gold standard is positive = false negative results on current exam If the current exam is positive but the gold standard is negative= false positive results on current exam If the current exam is negative and the gold standard is negative = true negative results on current exam If the current exam is positive and the gold standard is positive = true positive results on current exam

column1:
What is the most common location of an intracranial aneurysm?

A: Posterior communicating artery
B: Anterior communicating artery
C: Middle cerebral artery
D: Internal carotid artery siphon
column2:
Answer: B. Anterior communicating artery

Feedback:
The anterior communicating artery is the most common location for a berry or saccular intracranial aneurysm.

column1:
A patient presents with a history of recurrent upper extremity DVT due to Paget Schroetter syndrome. The referring physician ordered a PVR exam of the upper extremities instead of a venous Doppler exam of the upper extremities. Why?

A: to rule out Raynaud's syndrome
B: to rule out a subclavian steal
C: to rule out thoracic outlet syndrome
D: to rule out associated atherosclerotic disease
column2:
Answer: C. to rule out thoracic outlet syndrome

Feedback:
Paget Schroetter syndrome is commonly associated with thoracic outlet syndrome. The presence of a cervical rib or aberrant muscle band can be involved with the DVT formation with Paget Schroetter syndrome.

column1:
Which of the following Doppler findings would indicate significant venous hypertension?

A: Absence of flow in the deep veins on deep expiration
B: Increased systolic velocities with decreased diastolic velocities
C: Phasic flow patterns in the lower extremity deep veins
D: Triphasic flow patterns in the lower extremity deep veins
column2:
Answer: D. Triphasic flow patterns in the lower extremity deep veins

Feedback:
Significant pulsatility in the deep veins of the lower extremity indicates increased venous pressures (venous HTN). CHF, electrolyte imbalance and kidney dysfunction can lead to venous HTN. If the flow pattern normally seen in the IVC and hepatic veins is seen in the legs, venous HTN is present. Triphasic flow means the flow has 3 phases of movement in a single cardiac cycle. (The Doppler tracing crosses the baseline 3 times in a cardiac cycle.) The term is not limited to describing arterial flow. Continuous flow patterns in the veins can be a sign of chronic obstruction proximally.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes a screening exam?

A: testing used to monitor known disease progression
B: testing that leads to the initial diagnosis
C: testing used to determine the presence or absence of disease in an asymptomatic patient
D: the initial exam following vascular intervention/surgery that is usually done within first month after the procedure
column2:
Answer: C. testing used to determine the presence or absence of disease in an asymptomatic patient

Feedback:
Screening examination: testing used to determine the presence or absence of disease in an asymptomatic patient Baseline examination: testing that leads to the initial diagnosis or the initial exam following vascular intervention/surgery that is usually done within first month after the procedure Surveillance examination: testing used to monitor known disease progression; follow up exam timing is based on the time since initial diagnosis or revascularization

column1:
The image is a transverse view of the right antecubital fossa. What vessel is indicated by letter C?

A: basilic vein
B: brachial artery
C: brachial vein
D: cephalic vein
column2:
Answer: B. brachial artery

Feedback:
If the image is of the right arm, the left side of the image corresponds with the lateral right arm. The veins of the arm listed from lateral to medial: cephalic, brachial, basilic. The red color in the vessel labeled C indicates flow in the opposite direction of the other vessels on the screen. Letter C is the brachial artery.

column1:
If a liver transplant patient has a piggyback anastomosis, how does this affect your evaluation?

A: the hepatic artery anastomosis will not be able to be visualized because it is located deep within the liver tissue
B: there will be two anastomosis sites in the main portal vein that must be evaluated
C: there will be a single anastomosis site in the IVC that must be evaluated
D: each hepatic vein is connected to the native IVC separately, so there will be three anastomosis sites in the IVC that must be evaluated
column2:
Answer: C. there will be a single anastomosis site in the IVC that must be evaluated

Feedback:
Before beginning the exam, the surgical notes should be reviewed to determine if the patient has a full or partial transplant, a piggyback or interposition anastomosis and note the anastomosis sites for all vasculature. Piggyback - hepatic confluence of transplant liver attached to native IVC; single anastomosis site Interposition - donor IVC segment replaces a segment of the native IVC; two anastomosis sites

column1:
The vessel wall layer that is composed of fibrous connective tissue is called:

A: vasa vasorum
B: tunica externa
C: tunica media
D: tunica intima
column2:
Answer: B. tunica externa

Feedback:
The external layer is composed of connective tissue and adheres to the surrounding structures. The tunica media refers to the muscle layer of the vessel wall. The inner layer is the tunica intima which is composed of epithelial cells and covered by the basement membrane.

column1:
