Which of the following normal arteries is not palpable on physical exam?

A: posterior tibial
B: common femoral
C: dorsalis pedis
D: peroneal
column2:
Answer: D. peroneal

Feedback:
Because the location in the calf, the peroneal artery is not normally palpable.

column1:
Which of the following is a branch of the ICA?

A: Superior thyroidal
B: Temporal
C: Lingual
D: Ophthalmic
column2:
Answer: D. Ophthalmic

Feedback:
The ophthalmic artery feeds the eye and surrounding structures. This is the only branch of the ICA before it enters the Circle of Willis. The ECA is responsible for feeding extracranial structures including the face, portions of the throat and neck, tongue and scalp.

column1:
When you change the angle on the Doppler cursor, the ________________ also changes.

A: the volume of flow interrogated
B: speed of sound in tissue
C: flow velocity displayed
D: amplitude of the reflected signal
column2:
Answer: C. flow velocity displayed

Feedback:
Overestimating the angle between the beam and the blood flow = overestimated flow velocity Underestimating the angle between the beam and the blood flow = underestimated flow velocity

column1:
Vessel wall and plaque morphology are best evaluated in 2D when the beam is at what incident angle to the vessel?

A: 0 degrees
B: 45 degrees
C: 60 degrees
D: 90 degrees
column2:
Answer: D. 90 degrees

Feedback:
A perpendicular incidence of the beam to the vessel wall will provide the best reflection for the most accurate information display on the 2D image. Doppler flow information is most accurate when the beam is parallel (0 degrees) to the flow movement. The best obtainable Doppler angle is 60 degrees.

column1:
Why is the distance from the most proximal point of the contracted GSV to the saphenofemoral junction measured in a post-ablation exam?

A: to document the lack of thrombus propagation into the deep system
B: to assess the length of the segment that still requires treatment
C: to document that the procedure was unsuccessful
D: to assess the risk of future reflux in that area
column2:
Answer: A. to document the lack of thrombus propagation into the deep system

Feedback:
The distance between the SFJ and most proximal point of fibrosis/thrombosis of the GSV should be measured. If thrombus extends into the deep system, the GSV must be ligated because of the increased risk of embolization.

column1:
You obtain the PPG tracing from the second digit of the left hand. Which of the following is NOT an expected cause for the findings?

A: Thromboangiitis obliterans
B: Leriche syndrome
C: Raynaud disease
D: Thoracic outlet syndrome
column2:
Answer: B. Leriche syndrome

Feedback:
Leriche syndrome refers to stenosis or occlusion of the distal aorta. This would lead to a similar PPG tracing from the LOWER extremity digits.

column1:
Which of the following treatments would not be recommended for chronic peripheral arterial disease?

A: Bypass graft
B: Angioplasty
C: Coumadin therapy
D: Thrombolytic therapy
column2:
Answer: D. Thrombolytic therapy

Feedback:
Thrombolytic therapy is used to treat DVT or acute arterial occlusion by thrombus.

column1:
When calculating the ICA/CCA peak systolic velocity ratio,

A: use the lowest CCA PSV and the highest ICA PSV obtained
B: use the proximal CCA PSV and the ICA PSV obtained with the highest velocity
C: use the distal CCA PSV and the ICA PSV obtained with the highest velocity
D: use the proximal CCA PSV and the highest ICA PSV obtained from within the first 3cm of the vessel
column2:
Answer: C. use the distal CCA PSV and the ICA PSV obtained with the highest velocity

Feedback:
When calculating the ICA/CCA peak systolic velocity ratio, use the distal CCA PSV and the ICA PSV obtained with the highest velocity.

column1:
The flow demonstrated below the baseline is:

A: caused by incoming collateral flow due to the significant stenosis.
B: caused by turbulent flow at the site of the stenosis.
C: noise caused by patient respiration.
D: an example of cross talk artifact.
column2:
Answer: B. caused by turbulent flow at the site of the stenosis.

Feedback:
Eddy currents or vortices of swirling flow are seen just distal to a stenosis. These swirling currents of blood can be demonstrated as areas of antegrade and retrograde flow occurring simultaneously on the Doppler tracing.

column1:
When preparing for a patient with suspected thoracic outlet syndrome, what other information should you review for pertinent information?

A: Aortogram
B: Chest x-ray
C: Lab results for hematocrit levels
D: CT of the brain
column2:
Answer: B. Chest x-ray

Feedback:
A chest x-ray could demonstrate the cervical rib that is a common cause for TOS.

column1:
What term refers to the phenomenon of a PW Doppler tracing displaying a wider distribution of the velocity of blood cells with varying degrees of arterial blockage?

A: Spectral broadening
B: Occlusion
C: Turbulence
D: Laminar flow
column2:
Answer: A. Spectral broadening

Feedback:
Spectral broadening refers to PW Doppler tracing displaying a wider distribution of the velocity of blood cells with varying degrees of arterial blockage. Laminar flow refers to the slight difference in speed in the layers of blood within a vessel. An occlusion would not demonstrate any blood velocity on a PW Doppler tracing. Turbulence refers to the disruption of laminar flow causing eddy currents within the vessel. Turbulence is displayed as spectral broadening on a PW Doppler tracing.

column1:
Which of the following vessels typically supplies blood to a carotid body tumor?

A: branches of the internal carotid artery
B: branches of the middle cerebral artery
C: branches of the external carotid artery
D: branches of the subclavian artery
column2:
Answer: C. branches of the external carotid artery

Feedback:
Branches of the ECA typically supply blood to a carotid body tumor. Because the ICA has no extracranial branches, it is much less commonly the source of arterial flow to a carotid body tumor.

column1: Review the image and the system settings listed along the right side. Use your mouse to position your cursor over the setting that should be adjusted to improve the diagnostic quality of the Doppler tracing. Click to set the cursor and confirm your answer.
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
Doppler settings should always be adjusted to allow the waveform to fill the display area. This allows for proper display and measurement of waveform characteristics.

column1:
Which of the following is NOT an appropriate reason to terminate the treadmill exercise for a lower extremity arterial exam?

A: patient complains of leg pain
B: patient complains that the treadmill is too noisy
C: patient complains of shortness of breath
D: patient complains of chest pain
column2:
Answer: B. patient complains that the treadmill is too noisy

Feedback:
Anytime the patient reports symptoms of pain in the legs or the chest, the exam should be terminated. Never try to talk the patient into continuing the exam. Always record the duration of the exercise, location of the symptoms and changes in ankle pressures.

column1:
A 76yr old female with diabetes, HTN, and COPD is sent to the vascular lab for a six month follow up on a carotid stenosis. What is the best patient position to use for the exam?

A: Trendelenburg position
B: Semi-erect with or without a pillow
C: Seated upright on a stool beside the machine, facing you
D: Supine, neck slightly extended
column2:
Answer: B. Semi-erect with or without a pillow

Feedback:
If the patient has COPD, they should not lie flat on their back. They should be scanned in the semi-erect position, usually without a pillow to maintain a straight neck position. If the patient is seated on a stool, it will be difficult for them to hold steady for the exam and the Sonographer position would be improper ergonomically.

column1:
The pre and post exercise pressure information displayed from the right leg demonstrates which of the following?

A: Single level stenosis
B: Multiple level stenosis
C: Raynaud's Syndrome
D: Normal study
column2:
Answer: A. Single level stenosis

Feedback:
Single Level Obstruction-pressures return to normal 2-6 minutes post exercise Multi Level Obstruction-pressures return to normal 6-12 minutes post exercise

column1:
Which of the following parameters is displayed as the x-axis on a Doppler tracing?

A: time
B: motion
C: velocity
D: speed
column2:
Answer: A. time

Feedback:
Doppler shift frequencies are displayed on a tracing as a function of time; x axis = time, y axis = Doppler shift

column1:
______________________________ is defined as the time from onset of systole to the time peak systole occurs.

A: spatial pulse length
B: velocity time interval
C: acceleration time
D: resistive index
column2:
Answer: C. acceleration time

Feedback:
Acceleration time is defined as the time from onset of systole to the time peak systole occurs. It decreases as resistance increases. Increased resistance causes a short time between the onset of systole and peak systole.

column1:
If the stenosis/blockage reduces the vessel lumen to 1/2 of the normal vessel area, the velocity of flow at the stenosis must _________________to maintain the flow rate

A: double
B: increase by 200%
C: quadruple
D: decrease by 50%
column2:
Answer: A. double

Feedback:
If the stenosis/blockage reduces the lumen to 1/2 of the normal vessel area, the velocity of flow at the stenosis must double to maintain the flow rate.

column1:
When performing a segmental pressure exam on a patient with claudication, which of the following describes the response to exercise in a patient with pseudoclaudication?

A: Leg pain will not be associated with a drop in ankle pressure
B: ABI will most likely be 0.5-0.9
C: Leg pain will be associated with a drop in ankle pressure
D: ABI will most likely be <0.5
column2:
Answer: A. Leg pain will not be associated with a drop in ankle pressure

Feedback:
Pseudoclaudication has an orthopedic or neurogenic cause and is not caused by ischemia. The ABI will be normal and there will be no drop in ankle pressure with exercise.

column1:
Which of the following is a reason for lack of color fill in a transverse view of the distal femoral vein?

A: calcified plaque in the distal femoral artery
B: the presence of reflux
C: scale settings too low
D: filter settings too low
column2:
Answer: A. calcified plaque in the distal femoral artery

Feedback:
Significant calcification in the SFA will cause shadowing and limit the transmission of sound to tissues located deeper in the leg. The femoral vein lies posterior to the SFA and the artifact could inhibit color flow information in the vein. Low filter settings will allow the transducer to detect lower velocity signals such as venous flow. Low filter settings would IMPROVE venous flow visualization. Increasing the filter settings could degrade color fill on the image. Venous flow detection requires a low velocity scale. If a patient has reflux, the vein should be enlarged and flow easily detected.

column1:
If new research shows that the minimum velocity criteria used to diagnose >60% renal artery stenosis is actually 225cm/s, not 180cm/s, how will this affect the accuracy of the renal Doppler exam?

A: no effect on the accuracy, only affects positive and negative predictive value
B: decrease will vary with patient population
C: increase by 4.5%
D: increase will vary with patient population
column2:
Answer: D. increase will vary with patient population

Feedback:
Negative predictive value refers to the ability of an exam to correctly predict the absence of disease. Raising the peak velocity required to identify a significant renal stenosis will decrease the negative predictive value. This occurs due to the increased amount of patients that have a lower velocity but truly demonstrate >60% stenosis in the renal artery. The positive predictive value will increase for the renal Doppler exam because more patients will actually have significant stenosis when their exam produces velocities above 225cm/s. Accuracy will increase because of the increase in truly positive exams compared to the total number of exams. The more people correctly diagnosed as positive, the greater the accuracy of an exam.

column1:
Which of the following indicates the proper order for putting on personal protective equipment?

A: goggles, gown, mask, gloves
B: gloves, gown, mask, goggles
C: gown, mask, goggles, gloves
D: mask, goggles, gloves, gown
column2:
Answer: C. gown, mask, goggles, gloves

Feedback:
Before putting on PPE, always wash your hands first. When putting on PPE, the CDC recommends putting the gown on first, then the mask, followed by goggles and finish with the gloves.

column1:
What 2D artifact is demonstrated on the image?

A: edge shadowing/defocusing
B: edge enhancement
C: side lobes
D: mirror image
column2:
Answer: A. edge shadowing/defocusing

Feedback:
Caused by a reduction of reflection echoes by diffraction of the sound waves that touch the margin of a round boundary. Commonly seen in transverse views of blood vessels.

column1:
A diabetic patient presents for a segmental pressure exam. The calf and ankle vessels are non-compressible with up to 220mmHg. What alternative exam can be performed to evaluate lower extremity pressures?

A: Doppler ultrasound
B: photoplethysmography and toe cuffs
C: pulse volume recording exam
D: photoplethysmography with ankle cuffs
column2:
Answer: B. photoplethysmography and toe cuffs

Feedback:
The tiny vessels of the toes are usually spared from medial calcification. PPG waveforms and pressure measurements can be taken on the toes when the ankle pressures are falsely elevated.

column1:
Which of the following is an absolute contraindication to an endovenous ablation?

A: history of diabetes mellitus
B: current antibiotic use
C: daily aspirin use
D: uncooperative patient
column2:
Answer: D. uncooperative patient

Feedback:
An absolute contraindication is a situation which makes a particular treatment or procedure absolutely inadvisable. These include a known allergy to the injected material (ablation, steroid injection) , lack of appropriate equipment or skill to complete the procedure, uncooperative patient (uncontrolled movement during the procedure can result in injury, laceration or hemorrhage ) A relative contraindication is a condition which makes a particular treatment or procedure possibly inadvisable. Changes can be made to make the procedure safer or easier to perform. These include coagulopathy and the use of blood thinners, aspirin or antibiotics. If permitted by their physician, the patient can discontinue their blood thinner medication until their PT and INR values return to normal levels and then proceed with the invasive procedure. Warfarin, Coumadin, Jantoven, Xarelto, Pradaxa or Lovenox, Aspirin, Ibuprofen, NSAIDs should be stopped at least 7 days before a procedure and resumed 7 days after the procedure. Other relative contraindications include: Unsafe access route - must avoid large arteries and adjacent nerves Local infection, rash, or skin breakdown

column1:
You are performing a left upper extremity venous duplex exam. DVT is identified in the axillary and subclavian veins. Which of the following must also be included in the exam protocol for this patient?

A: flow in the contralateral subclavian vein
B: arterial flow pattern in the arteries associated with the thrombosed veins
C: bilateral brachial pressures
D: pulse volume recording of the ipsilateral upper arm and forearm
column2:
Answer: A. flow in the contralateral subclavian vein

Feedback:
For all unilateral extremity venous exams, the flow in the contralateral extremity should be documented for comparison. Common femoral vein for the lower extremities and subclavian or internal jugular vein for the upper extremities.

column1:
Abnormal results for a penile Doppler evaluation that suggest impotence include _______________ peak systolic flow velocity and a peak pressure ____________.

A: >30cm/s, >60mmHg
B: <60cm/s, >60mmHg
C: >60cm/s, >30mmHg
D: <30cm/s, <60mmHg
column2:
Answer: D. <30cm/s, <60mmHg

Feedback:
Abnormal results for a penile Doppler evaluation that suggest impotence include <30cm/s peak systolic flow velocity and a peak pressure <60mmHg.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes the positive predictive value (PPV) of a testing technique?

A: The ability of a test to detect disease when it IS present.
B: The ability of a test to rule out disease when it IS NOT present.
C: If the test result is positive, PPV is the probability that the patient actually has the disease
D: If the test result is negative, PPV is the probability that the patient does not have disease
column2:
Answer: C. If the test result is positive, PPV is the probability that the patient actually has the disease

Feedback:
Positive Predictive Value-Calculates how often a positive study is correctly diagnosed; If the test result is positive, PPV is the probability that the patient actually has the disease

column1:
Which of the following is a contraindication for a lower extremity segmental pressure exam?

A: ulceration on the 3rd and 4th toes
B: current anticoagulant treatment
C: recent saphenous vein arterial bypass grafting
D: diabetes mellitus
column2:
Answer: C. recent saphenous vein arterial bypass grafting

Feedback:
Contraindications: Suspected or known acute deep venous thrombosis - use waveform analysis only or use of a toe/brachial index (TBI) Recent surgery, trauma site or ulcers that cannot/should not be compressed by pressure cuffs History of lower limb stent replacement, or recent saphenous vein arterial bypass grafting Patients with incompressible arteries due to medial calcification - TBI may be used instead Morbidly obese patients - high-thigh pressure may be unobtainable or inaccurate due to limb girth Patients with significant tremors or involuntary movements that may cause waveforms to be suboptimal or unreliable Ulcerations on the 3rd and 4th toes would not be compressed by cuffs. While diabetes mellitus can cause medial calcification which could inhibit the exam, not every patient with DM has medial calcification.

column1:
According to the findings on this arteriogram, which of the following would NOT be an expected symptom related to the findings?

A: Left arm paresthesia
B: Right leg paralysis
C: Visual disturbances of the left eye
D: Left leg paresthesia
column2:
Answer: D. Left leg paresthesia

Feedback:
There is a significant stenosis of the left CCA and an occlusion of the proximal left subclavian artery. The left CCA flow velocities will be elevated with diminished flow distally. The left vertebral artery is not demonstrated due to the proximal subclavian obstruction. NO steal is present or these vessels would be identified. Occlusion of the left subclavian = left arm paresthesia Diminished left CCA flow = right leg paralysis (left side of brain controls right side of body) and left eye symptoms There is no explanation for left leg paresthesia on this image. This symptom would require right CCA/ICA blockage if it was related to abnormal cerebrovascular circulation. ***Image Credit Bronchiectasis and hoarseness of voice in takayasu arteritis: a rare presentation. Nd Perera G, C Jayasinghe A, D Dias L, Kulatunga A - BMC Res Notes (2012)

column1:
What console controls should be adjusted to improve the image?

A: increase the color Doppler scale
B: increase the PW Doppler scale
C: drop the baseline
D: more than one of the above
column2:
Answer: D. more than one of the above

Feedback:
This image can be improved in multiple ways. There is color bleed outside the vessel on the color image. This indicates that the color scale is too low and should be increased. The waveform should fill the PW Doppler tracing area. The PW Doppler scale should be reduced to enlarge the waveform. The retrograde component of the flow is estimated at 50cm/s maximum velocity. There is too much space under the baseline for the flow to be displayed. The baseline dropped for proper placement of the waveforms in the display area.

column1:
Which of the following is a critical finding that requires immediate attention?

A: recanalization of the great saphenous vein after chemical ablation
B: dilated hepatic veins in a liver transplant patient
C: acute occlusion of the external iliac artery in a renal transplant patient
D: echogenic walls and posterior shadowing from a hepatoportal shunt that was inserted 2 days ago
column2:
Answer: C. acute occlusion of the external iliac artery in a renal transplant patient

Feedback:
An acute arterial occlusion of any artery is a critical finding but especially in renal transplant patients. Acute occlusion of the renal artery or supplying artery can cause significant damage or loss of the allograft. Most renal transplant arteries are connected to the external iliac artery. An acute occlusion of this artery not only puts the leg at risk of damage, but the renal allograft as well.

column1:
An upper extremity venous Doppler that is performed for suspected DVT should be performed with the patient in the ____________________ position.

A: prone
B: Trendelenburg
C: standing
D: supine
column2:
Answer: D. supine

Feedback:
Patient should be evaluated in the supine position with the head turned slightly away from the affected side; the affected arm should be in the dependent position and externally rotated.

column1:
Which of the following would be the probe frequency of choice for a TCD exam?

A: 2MHz
B: 4MHz
C: 7MHz
D: 10MHz
column2:
Answer: A. 2MHz

Feedback:
TCD requires a low MHz probe to penetrate the skull.

column1:
Which of the following is the most likely explanation for findings displayed on the image?

A: The normal subclavian artery is displayed with significant reflux demonstrated in the subclavian vein
B: The deeper vessel is a mirror image artifact caused by the apex of the lung
C: The subclavian artery is duplicated and both vessels demonstrate normal flow
D: The superficial vessel is a mirror image artifact caused by the apex of the lung
column2:
Answer: B. The deeper vessel is a mirror image artifact caused by the apex of the lung

Feedback:
The deeper vessel is a mirror image artifact caused by the apex of the lung. The lung apex is located immediately posterior to the subclavian artery and acts as a highly reflective acoustic interface that causes the mirror image artifact. Mirror image artifacts cause the incorrect display of a duplicate structure deep to the actual structure. The subclavian artery could possibly be duplicated but it is very rare.

column1:
Which of the following is a required part of the Sonographer's preliminary report for a lower extremity segmental pressure exam?

A: patient position during the exam
B: systolic and diastolic pressure measurements
C: peak velocity of the flow in the arteries
D: duration of cuff inflation during the exam
column2:
Answer: A. patient position during the exam

Feedback:
If the patient is unable to be examined in the supine position, this must be documented on the preliminary report for the interpreting physician because the pressures obtained may be artifactually elevated. The systolic pressure measurements are obtained and reported, but no diastolic pressures are obtained in the legs. Flow velocity is not reported on a segmental pressure exam. It is an important part of an arterial duplex exam.

column1:
If a patient complains of a single 12hr episode of right arm paresthesia and paralysis, what will you report on your technologist worksheet?

A: history of transient ischemic attack
B: history of cerebrovascular accident
C: history of reversible ischemic neurological defect
D: history of amaurosis fugax
column2:
Answer: A. history of transient ischemic attack

Feedback:
Symptoms that last less than 24hrs = TIA.

column1:
When evaluating the left arm for potential placement of a central venous catheter, you identify multiple small venous collaterals surrounding the subclavian vein. What should you do next?

A: evaluate the extremity arteries instead for potential catheterization
B: evaluate the venous system in the right arm
C: measure the largest collateral for potential pathway for the catheter
D: tell the physician the patient is not a candidate for catheter placement
column2:
Answer: B. evaluate the venous system in the right arm

Feedback:
If venous collaterals are identified on the pre-procedure scan for central venous catheter placement, there is a stenosis or occlusion of the adjacent major vein. The patency and size of the collaterals are insignificant when deciding not to use that extremity for the catheter. The mere presence of the collaterals indicates an issue with that venous pathway. Normal veins are preferred for catheter placement. These findings are usually going to change the access point or placement of the central venous catheter to another vein/arm, but the Sonographer does not advise the physician on that decision. The opposite arm should be evaluated for an alternate location for catheter placement. Arteries cannot be used for the same purpose as veins for catheter delivery of medications.

column1:
According to the Appropriate Use Criteria, which of the following is an appropriate indication for an lower extremity venous duplex exam for venous insufficiency?

A: Positive D-dimer test
B: Suspected or diagnosed pulmonary hypertension
C: Hyperpigmentation
D: Telangiectasia
column2:
Answer: C. Hyperpigmentation

Feedback:
Hyperpigmentation of the distal calf occurs with chronic venous insufficiency. The waste products in the venous blood that pools in the calf are forced into the subcutaneous tissues by osmosis. Telangiectasia refers to dilated venules that appear as tiny red/purple veins. They are also called spider veins. While these tiny veins can indicate venous stasis, their isolated appearance has not been associated with insufficiency in the larger superficial veins.

column1:
Which upper extremity vein is most commonly evaluated for use as an arterial bypass?

A: median cubital vein
B: brachial vein
C: radial vein
D: cephalic vein
column2:
Answer: D. cephalic vein

Feedback:
The cephalic vein is most commonly harvested for use as a bypass.

column1:
A common complication of cardiac catheterization is the formation of:

A: Fusiform aneurysm
B: Arterial dissection
C: DVT
D: Pseudoaneurysm
column2:
Answer: D. Pseudoaneurysm

Feedback:
During the catheterization the superficial femoral artery is punctured by the catheter as it enters the arterial system. Once the catheter is removed, there is a risk that blood will escape the artery into the surrounding tissues to form a pseudoaneurysm.

column1:
Why did the technologist steer the 2D image while scanning during the thrombin injection?

A: to improve the color flow demonstration
B: to make the US beam more perpendicular to the long axis of the needle
C: to make the US beam more parallel to the long axis of the needle
D: to provide a better view of the posterior enhancement from the pseudoaneurysm
column2:
Answer: B. to make the US beam more perpendicular to the long axis of the needle

Feedback:
The more perpendicular the US beam is to the long axis of the needle, the easier it is to visualize due to increased reflection.
